---
title: "cravings reading and log"
format: html
---

## Cravings Reading and Log

Cravings are a normal part of recovery and do not mean you are failing. They are time-limited body and mind responses linked to cues, memories, and learned routines.

An urge can feel urgent, but it is not permanent. Most urges rise, peak, and pass. The key skill is delaying action and choosing a response that keeps you safe until intensity drops.

When you track cravings, patterns become clearer: times of day, emotional states, social contexts, and thought habits. This information helps you prepare instead of react.

Cravings are influenced by learning, context, and emotional state. That means they can be understood and managed, even when they feel intense. The strongest part of an urge is usually brief; what changes outcomes is how you respond during that window.

This activity builds two protective skills: early recognition (spotting triggers and escalation) and response flexibility (choosing actions that reduce risk). Practicing these skills repeatedly lowers fear of cravings and increases confidence in your ability to stay sober under pressure.

::: callout
### Reflection Questions

1.  Which cue triggers me most often?
2.  What thought appears first when a craving starts?
3.  What has helped me ride out an urge before?
:::

| Time | Trigger | Urge intensity (0-10) | What I did | Intensity after 10 min |
|------|---------|-----------------------|------------|------------------------|
|      |         |                       |            |                        |
|      |         |                       |            |                        |
|      |         |                       |            |                        |
|      |         |                       |            |                        |
